RN-Vollprüfungen legen Wert auf Priorisierung, instabile Kunden und Delegation; PN-Vollprüfungen legen Wert auf stabile Kunden, Aufgaben und Umfang. RN/NP-Abonnenten können über die Stufenleiter weiterhin die vollständige PN-Praxis eröffnen.

learner.account.readiness.title: Passing probability & readiness
learner.account.readiness.intro: A blended signal from lessons, graded practice, and mocks — indicative only, not a pass guarantee.
learner.account.readiness.empty: Complete more graded question bank or mock sessions to unlock a sharper readiness picture.
learner.readinessPage.catNone: No completed CAT-style practice tests yet. When you finish adaptive runs, we show accuracy here for context (the headline index still uses mocks and bank practice in its formula).
learner.readinessPage.catSummary: {n} completed CAT session(s). Average accuracy where scored: {pct}%.
Why this feels different from a question-only bank
This index blends pathway lesson progress, graded bank and mock performance, and topic load from your weak areas—so it reflects study and practice together, not isolated quiz scores.
learner.readinessPage.confidenceExplaine: Confidence reflects how much graded practice and mock history we have, not clinical certainty.
learner.readinessPage.consistencyNote: Mock score spread across attempts is folded into the mock factor when you have multiple exams — wide swings temper the headline index.
learner.readinessPage.formula.lessons: Lesson completion vs lessons available in your plan pool.
learner.readinessPage.formula.mocks: Recent mock exams (attempts with at least five scored items each).
learner.readinessPage.formula.practice: Accuracy on graded items from recent completed question-bank and exam sessions (your plan scope).
learner.readinessPage.formula.renormaliz: If a signal is missing (for example, no mocks yet), its weight is left out and the index is renormalized so you are not penalized with unexplained empty points.
learner.readinessPage.formula.topics: Topic error load from priority weak areas (ledger + sessions).
learner.readinessPage.indexFootnote: This number is a NurseNest readiness index (0–100), not a published passing probability.
learner.readinessPage.noGradedPractice: No graded bank items in recent completed sessions yet.
learner.readinessPage.noMocks: No mock attempts on file yet.
learner.readinessPage.section.catSub: Adaptive (CAT) practice tests you have completed — shown for transparency; the headline score above does not add a separate CAT weight.
learner.readinessPage.section.consistenc: Study streak and how mock variability affects the mock portion of your index.
learner.readinessPage.section.formulaSub: Matches the server-side blend in `computeReadiness` — weights apply when each signal qualifies.
learner.readinessPage.section.mocksSub: Newest scored mocks (same pool used in the mock factor when eligible).
learner.readinessPage.section.signalsSub: Point contributions after your data qualifies for each signal.
learner.readinessPage.section.weakSub: From unified topic performance. Use questions for targeted drills; lessons for structured review.
learner.readinessPage.streakCaption: Consecutive calendar days (UTC) with any study signal in the last 90 days.

learner.account.security.title: Security & password
learner.account.security.intro: Keep your NurseNest password strong. OAuth-only accounts can set a password via email reset.
learner.security.section.passwordSub: Uses your current password. After a successful change you will be signed out here and should sign in again.
learner.security.section.resetSub: Same secure flow as “Forgot password” on the sign-in page.
learner.security.resetBody: If you cannot remember your password, we will email a one-time link. The link expires after a short time. If you sign in with Google or another provider only, use this once to create a NurseNest password.
learner.security.sessionExplain: NurseNest keeps you signed in with an encrypted browser cookie (about {{days}} days). It is not shared with other sites. We do not show a list of devices or browsers.
learner.security.sessionOtherDevices: After you change your password, this browser signs out automatically. On any other device or browser, open NurseNest and choose Sign out if you no longer want that session, or simply sign in again when the old session expires.
learner.security.trouble1: Confirm you are using the same email you registered with, or your username if you set one.
learner.security.trouble2: Try an incognito or private window to rule out extensions or old cookies.
learner.security.trouble3: Request a new reset link from the forgot-password page; older links stop working after use or when they expire.
learner.security.trouble4: Make sure your device clock is roughly correct; extreme skew can break some secure flows.
learner.security.noPasswordBefore: This account does not use a NurseNest password yet. Use
learner.security.noPasswordAfter: with your email to set one.
learner.security.passwordPolicyHint: Use at least 8 characters with a letter and a number.

tools.transfusionSafety.intro: Tap the best immediate action. Rationales summarize what to watch for on exams and on the unit.
tools.transfusionSafety.metaDescription: Practice identifying transfusion reactions and first-line nursing responses.
tools.transfusionSafety.opt2a: Rising free hemoglobin or plasma hemoglobin with decreasing haptoglobin (with clinical correlation).
tools.transfusionSafety.opt2b: Isolated platelet count drop without other signs.
tools.transfusionSafety.optA: Stop the transfusion, keep IV access with normal saline, and notify the provider and blood bank per protocol.
tools.transfusionSafety.optB: Slow the infusion to 50 mL/hr and continue monitoring.
tools.transfusionSafety.optC: Give antipyretics and continue the transfusion if vital signs stabilize.
tools.transfusionSafety.q1: A patient develops fever, chills, and flank pain 45 minutes into a red blood cell transfusion. What is your priority?
tools.transfusionSafety.q2: You suspect acute hemolytic transfusion reaction. Which lab pair best supports hemolysis in this context?
tools.transfusionSafety.rationale1: Febrile or hemolytic suspicion requires stopping the blood product, maintaining patent IV access with saline, monitoring, and escalation—before restarting anything.
tools.transfusionSafety.rationale2: Hemolysis releases free hemoglobin and consumes haptoglobin; always interpret with the clinical picture and institutional transfusion reaction pathway.
tools.transfusionSafety.title: Transfusion safety
